methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Quantum Computing

data.narrative:
Pasqal is a pre-IPO quantum computing company founded by Alain Aspect and Georges-Olivier Reymond, having raised €340M+ to date. The quantum computing sector comprises a limited public comparable universe of three traded firms (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) alongside eight private peers including IonQ, Quantinuum, Rigetti, D-Wave, and Infleqtion.

Traditional peer-median valuation methodology is inapplicable here. The quantum computing sector lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables meeting minimum clustering thresholds; zero same-stage peers and zero adjacent-stage peers exist within comparable valuation tiers, falling short of the required three-datapoint minimum. Relying on all-sector medians would introduce material distortion given the heterogeneity in quantum architectures, commercialization timelines, and capital intensity across the peer set.

The analysis therefore cannot support a point-estimate valuation or narrow range. Key uncertainties precluding triangulation include: (1) absence of revenue or profitability data for Pasqal and peer firms, limiting revenue multiple approaches; (2) highly fragmented quantum hardware technology roadmaps, rendering architectural comparability weak; (3) extreme early-stage nature of the sector, where valuations reflect speculative cash-burn multiples rather than fundamental metrics. Further disclosure of Pasqal's technical differentiation, customer pipeline, and burn rate would be required to construct meaningful scenario-based valuation bands.
